Notice Of Motion And Motion To Set Trial; Memorandum And Declaration In Support
Off Calendar Aug-19-2025 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Zoe Gallagher and Amit Rana for Annie Yi Tang, in her capacity as Administrator.
Kevin Rodriguez for Annie Tang and Betty Bong, in their individual capacities (via Zoom).
Audrey J. Grossman and Alan Yoshitake for Annie Tang and Betty Bong, as Trustees (via Zoom).
Robert Cross and Stephen Ruben for Mary Bong, Petitioner.

Ms. Gallagher and Mr. Rodriguez challenged the court's tentative decision to grant Mary Bong's Motion to Set Trial.

Ms. Gallagher and Mr. Rodriguez both argued that the trial should proceed only on the Second Amended Petition, not the Third, due to concerns over newly raised claims, inadequate time for discovery, and potential prejudice to respondents. Counsel emphasized that the Third Amended Petition significantly expanded the scope of the litigation with new claims and parties. They argued that these new claims were pled only in April 2025, giving respondents insufficient time to prepare for trial set in December 2025.

Respondents urged the Court to bifurcate the issues, allowing trial to proceed on the Second Amended Petition while requiring petitioner to file a separate petition for the newly added claims, thereby avoiding prejudice and preserving respondents' rights to pretrial motions and discovery.

Mr. Cross opposed bifurcation, arguing that all of the petitions arise from the same set of facts and that judicial efficiency would be undermined by separate trials. Mr. Cross contended that respondents had long been on notice of the claims now pled in the Third Amended Petition and had been offered stipulations to extend the five-year period, which they refused. He further stated that many of the factual issues were central to both the Second and Third Amended Petitions and could not practically be severed.

After hearing oral argument, the Court took the matter under submission and advised that a written order would be issued.

MINI MINUTES:

Appearances: Catherine Swafford for Annie Tang (Petitioner/Respondent).
Richard Shearer for Mary Bong (Petitioner/Objector).

Mr. Shearer stated that they have communicated, that his Client is willing to accept Debra Dolch to serve as the Administrator, that Ms. Swafford's Client would like to be the Administrator and that his Client is very much not amenable to having Ms. Swafford appointed. Ms. Swafford stated that her Client wants to proceed with being appointed, but if the Court is not inclined to do so, her Client will nominate Debra Dolch. The Court noted that it has not seen a petition to appoint Debra Dolch. Ms. Swafford stated that when the petitions were originally filed, they were unsure as to what was going to be happening in Hong Kong, that her Client is now appointed in Hong Kong, that Hong King found that her Client is qualified to serve and is competent, and that Hong Kong adjudicated that Mr. Bong was a domicile of Hong Kong. Ms. Swafford also stated that the only thing in California is a small ancillary administration, that there is $500,000.00 in a bank account, that all of the other California assets are assets of Mr. Bong's trust and that it makes sense to appoint her Client. In addition, Ms. Swafford stated that there is now a proceeding in Shanghai, that there may be one in Taiwan and that her Client is the Decedent's surviving Spouse. Mr. Shearer stated that given the very significant conflict in facts and what it happening to the assets, he is requesting that a Neutral be appointed. Ms. Swafford stated that her Client is handling the trust here and the estate taxes. The Petitions for Letters of Administration are under submission.
